---
title: "MB Habituation - Report"
output: html_document
---

## Functional Form of Habituation

**Question**. Across the pooled ManyBabies (MB1, MB3, MB4) habituation trials, 
what functional form best describes the looking time (LT)?

### What we compared

Habituation is a process in which the looking time of infants drops as the same 
stimulus is repeated. We are examining three forms for the effect of `trial_num`:

- **Linear** — a constant decrease per trial. 
- **Quadratic** (`poly(trial_num, 2)`) — allows curvature, so the slope can
  decelerate (or accelerate) across trials. 
- **Log** (`log(trial_num)`) — a sharp early drop that flattens into a long
  tail. When paired with `log(LT)` on the outcome side this is the
  **power law** `LT = a · trial_num^b`, which is the canonical habituation
  curve in the developmental literature (Bashinski, Werner & Rudy 1985;
  ManyBabies 1).

Each form was crossed with two outcome scales (raw LT and log LT) and with /
without a `trial_type` interaction, giving 12 mixed-effects models in total.
Every model included random intercepts and random slopes of `trial_num`
nested within `lab_id / subject`, and a fixed interaction with `project_id`
to absorb project-level baseline differences. The fits are produced in
[1_modeling.rmd](1_modeling.rmd).

### How we chose between them

We ranked models by **BIC**. 

### Result

The log form — specifically, `log(looking_time) ~ log(trial_num) * project_id * trial_type`
— had the **lowest BIC of all twelve candidates**. Quadratic models came in second within each DV/condition
block but were consistently dominated by the log form. The linear form was the
worst among all.
